paragraphs.text: An Coimisiún um Rialáil Cumarsáide Commission for Communications Regulation

section_path: Submission to European Commission call for evidence on Digital Fairness Act
Submission to European Commission call for evidence on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: An Coimisiún um Rialáil Cumarsáide Commission for Communications Regulation 1 Lárcheantar na nDugaí, Sráid na nGildeanna, BÁC 1, Éire, D01 E4X0. One Dockland Central, Guild Street, Dublin 1, Ireland, D01 E4X0. Teil | Tel +353 1 804 9600 Suíomh | Web www.comreg.ie

1.1 The Commission for Communications Regulation ('ComReg') welcomes the opportunity to respond to the European Commission's ('EC') public consultation and call for evidence on the EC's legislative initiative to enhance consumer protection and digital fairness through the Digital Fairness Act ('DFA'). ComReg also inputs to the work of the Body of European Regulators for Electronic Communications ('BEREC') and it has contributed to the submission made by BEREC to the EC's call for evidence on the DFA and supports the messages set out in that submission.

1.2 ComReg is the statutory body responsible for the regulation of the electronic communications sector (telecommunications, radio communications, broadcasting transmission and premium rate services) and the postal sector in Ireland. It has a range of functions and objectives in relation to the provision of Electronic Communications Networks and Services ('ECN' and 'ECS') in accordance with European Union ('EU') and national (Irish) legislation.

1.3 ECN and ECS play a vital role in Ireland's economy and wider society. These services are woven into the fabric of all our lives helping us to communicate, socialise, stream media content, and do business. Our reliance on these networks continues to grow, now and into the future, as they increasingly support different aspects of our domestic and work lives. They are now a critical part of the efficient function of consumers' work and social needs.

1.4 Regarding the scope and application of Directive (EU) 2018/1972 ('EECC'), ComReg highlights that the definition of ECS created by the EECC now comprises three categories of ECS, based on a functional approach. The provisions of the EECC can apply to Over-The-Top ('OTT') online services to the extent that they are functionally equivalent and enable communication. The provisions of the EECC can also apply to providers of ECN and ECS who additionally provide communications services, such as content (to the extent that they are functionally equivalent and enable communication), to end-users over the internet. Providers' services may fall into one of the three categories of ECS and may be subject to ECS sector specific rules which arise from the EECC as well as consumer protection legislation, for example, the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive
Page 3 of 12
Page 4 of 12
('UCPD'), which has been transposed into Irish law through the Consumer Protection Act 2007, as amended and the Consumer Rights Act, 2022, as amended.

1.5 ComReg’s mission is to implement effective regulation that supports the delivery of widespread, modern and resilient digital infrastructure, including communications networks, products, and services which, ultimately, benefit users.

1.6 Ensuring a high level of consumer protection and fairness for ECS consumers, including vulnerable customers is a continuing priority for ComReg. ComReg protects and informs consumers so that any harms are overcome and they can choose and use communications services with confidence. In addition, ComReg welcomes innovation and investment by providers in products and services promoting competition, ultimately to the benefit of consumers.

1.7 This document is submitted as ComReg’s response to the EC’s public consultation and call for evidence on the DFA. Section 2 of this document comments on the experiences and behaviours of ECS consumers in the context of some of the themes as set out by the EC (unfair commercial practices, transparency and properly informed consumers, right of exit from contracts etc.) and provides, at a high-level, some of ComReg’s experience in dealing with harmful practices faced by ECS consumers that exhibit some parallels with the harms highlighted in the EC’s Digital Fairness Fitness Check ('DFFC'). Section 3 comments on what ComReg considers to be key policy implications vis-à-vis the legislative initiative on digital fairness, such as it is.
Page 5 of 12

2.1 The technological advances in electronic communications give us unprecedented access to new services and content, including social media. ComReg wants to ensure that technological developments are utilised in a way so that consumers get maximum benefit from these developments in a competitive and a well-functioning ECS market and digital ecosystem.

2.2 However, it is recognised that consumer decision making can be significantly influenced by choice architecture employed by traders (including by ECS providers) both online and offline. Psychological biases and cognitive limitations may be systematically exploited by traders to the detriment of consumers. There are specific characteristics of electronic communications that lead to complexity in consumer decision making, for example, rapid technological change. Consumers may misjudge or be misled on their requirements (e.g. usage and speed) relative to their allowances (minutes, texts and megabytes) and therefore make sub optimal purchasing or use decisions.

2.3 It is noted that the benefit derived from a product or service used in the context of an ECS may be intangible; it is not simply the use of the handset or internet access service ('IAS') for example that is the benefit, but what the ECS allows one to do, i.e., communication, entertainment etc. As noted above, the industry is changing rapidly making the environment uncertain, thus predictions about usage are hard to make and require the decision making of consumers to be frequent.

2.4 Complexity in electronic communications decision making, amongst other things, likely means that consumer behavioural tendencies or decision-making biases can have a strong impact in the ECS sector compared to other consumer markets. While consumers differ in their attitudes and behaviours, some consumers are more likely to make sub-optimal choices (depending on the extent to which they are manipulated by external forces). In the ECS sector, behavioural bias can manifest itself in consumers choosing products and services that may not suit their needs or failing to make switching decisions that would save money or increase other benefits such as quality of service.

2.5 In this light, ComReg broadly agrees with the findings of the EC's DFFC with respect to the possible harmful practices (such as, harmful online choice architecture ("dark patterns") and unfair contract terms) highlighted therein. Broadly, such harmful practices if engaged in by providers or traders of ECS or Premium Rate Services ('PRS') may manipulate the purchasing decisions of consumers and may cause considerable detriment to consumers. ComReg

considers that potential harmful practices can be exacerbated in the digital environment – in light of complexity such as, the purchase of bundled offers or 'add-ons' and the increasing multiplicity (and in some cases lack of transparency) of players in the value chain.

2.6 ComReg notes the DFFC defines dark patterns as 'unfair commercial practices deployed through the structure, design or functionalities of digital interfaces or system architecture that can influence consumers to make decisions they would not have taken otherwise'. ComReg also notes that dark patterns are not defined under the DFA legislative initiative thus far. ComReg would welcome further consideration of this factor under the DFA.

2.7 In relation to ECS and PRS, ComReg has received complaints through the Consumer Care channel and compliance activities demonstrating harmful practices akin to those set out in the DFFC and covered in the EC's questionnaire on the DFA.

2.8 In the context of some of the themes set out (unfair commercial practices, transparency and properly informed consumers, right of exit from contracts etc.) some of ComReg's experience in dealing with harmful practices faced by consumers of ECS and PRS have parallels with the harms highlighted in the DFFC and these are set out:

Dark patterns in the ECS sector and PRS typically arise in the context of the way traders sign up customers and a lack of transparency of information and presentation of information. ComReg has experience of investigating complaints from consumers where providers were alleged to have adopted misleading opt-in mechanisms which resulted in consumers automatically opting into additional services at an extra cost. Furthermore, dark patterns can disproportionately impact persons with disabilities in comparison to the wider population. For example, the so-called Roach Motel dark pattern whereby a user signs up for a service then finds it difficult to cancel or unsubscribe can present a particular difficulty for people who are hard of hearing who sign up for a service online and are then required to contact the provider via the phone to cancel their subscription. Complex or confusing navigation systems, often a feature of dark patterns, can create major barriers for individuals with cognitive impairments or learning difficulties.

Where the specific features and design of a service exploit consumer behaviours or biases (e.g., inattention, inertia) then it is not beneficial and may be harmful to consumers. The potential for consumer detriment is clear where potential harmful practices, such as, scrolling and autoplay, keep consumers engaged with the ECS or PRS service where they would otherwise not do so. It may lead to recurring charges or cancellation charges. ComReg would welcome further consideration of the scope of consumer protection rules in
Page 6 of 12
relation to addictive design/specific features in digital products, and to future proof consumer protection.

Regarding informed decision making and informed consent, ComReg notes that existing consumer protection law (UCPD) protects consumers against unfair commercial practices giving them the right to be properly informed before purchasing ECS and PRS services.

ComReg recognises that some personalisation practices may have positive effects for consumers (such as, personalisation where it makes information more relevant to the ECS customers and assists their decision making). For example, the provisions of the EECC require providers of ECS to provide Best Tariff Advice/Best Tariff Information annually, and usage monitoring and control facility (such as, for example, a personalised online account and/or account via mobile applications).

ComReg considers that, if designed correctly, there can be consumer benefit to personalised usage, control facilities and consumption notifications. In particular, they should assist consumers to manage their spending by enabling them to make better informed decisions, such as to continue to use their service(s) and thus incur out-of-bundle charges, to stop using services, or to take time to consider changing or upgrading their individual tariff plan. These provisions focus on delivering the desired outcome for consumers in terms of enabling them to make informed decisions (including on the basis of complete contractual and usage information regarding their provider's electronic communications services and products which best meet their needs) and engaging with the switching process tenets of the EECC. However, where the personalised information or content is presented in a way or with the preliminary purpose of exploiting consumer behaviours or biases then it is not beneficial and may be harmful to consumers.

Regarding potential data protection and privacy matters, traders of ECS and PRS should be mindful of requirements under the GDPR and ePrivacy law when offering and processing data for the purposes of offering products and services.

The potential for consumer detriment is clear where marketing related to pricing includes misleading promotions, hidden fees, and lack of transparency. Such unfair marketing practices would lead to significant negative consequences for consumers, competition, and trust in traders of ECS and PRS and their service or ECS/PRS more generally a key tenet of the EECC. In that context, ComReg is concerned about practices, such as, advertising packages as "unlimited" when they are not in fact unlimited, offering a price
Page 7 of 12

"for life" or "forever" while subsequently changing the way the price was calculated and consequently the price itself. ComReg is of the view that where providers publish information describing the tariffs and communication units included in services as "unlimited", "for life" or "forever" or use these phrases in their advertising material and contractual terms to describe the nature and tariff of the services offered, that these terms could lead to confusion amongst end-users if those services are not, in fact, unlimited, "for life" or "forever" and may be harmful to consumers, contrary to requirements under the EECC and Consumer Protection Act, 2007 (as amended) in respect of unfair or misleading commercial practices.

ComReg notes that reference to digital contracts is governed by EU Directive 2019/770 which lays down common rules on certain requirements concerning contracts between vendors and consumers for the supply of digital content or digital services. ComReg considers that ECS consumers should be protected at a comparable level online as they are offline/in-person. Existing EU rules (Consumer Rights Directive and the EECC) require that Pre-Contractual Information for ECS contracts is provided to consumers on a “durable medium”, at a point before a consumer is bound by a contract. As set out in its regulatory guidance on Title III: End-User Rights of the EECC, ComReg is of the view that the Pre-Contractual Information on a durable medium must be personally addressed to the consumer, be capable of being retained for future reference and be in a format that can be reproduced in an unchanged format at a later point in time. Otherwise, the potential for detriment is clear where a customer does not receive their contract on a durable medium, such as, a lack of clarity of terms and conditions in consumer contracts and creating difficulty in cancelling the contract.

In terms of practical application of what constitutes provision on a durable medium, ComReg is of the view that sending a consumer a link which displays the Pre-Contractual Information required solely on the webpage of a provider’s website would not constitute a durable medium.

ComReg regularly receives consumer complaints in relation to contracts. To safeguard consumers, ComReg is of the view that providers should, in addition to highlighting the importance of downloading the document and retaining it for future reference, ensure that the means of downloading the document provided to the consumer (such as a hyperlink etc.) remains accessible. The European Accessibility Act requires that information provided to consumers about the accessibility and functioning of a service is available via more than one sensory channel. This may require, in certain circumstances, that information is provided in non-digital formats.
Page 8 of 12

As discussed above, ComReg recognises that specific features and design of the service (of ECS and PRS) may make it difficult and costly for consumers to leave the service, even when they want to. It may lead to consumers facing obstacles in cancellation (such as, a lack of transparency about cancellation, difficult navigation, digital literacy) of a service /contract and disincentives to switch (such as, persistent retention activity if the customer is set on cancelling). The potential for consumer detriment is clear where such harmful practices may arise for example in relation to charges or contracts.

As regards subscription services, ComReg continues to monitor developments in PRS markets, including new direct carrier billing (DCB) services, to ensure that consumers are not subscribed as a result of their first engagement with the PRS. As required by ComReg's PRS Code of Practice, consumers must be presented with the appropriate information about the subscription service, including on pricing, prior to confirming their subscription and throughout their use of PRS. In addition, the requirements of the PRS Code includes the subscription confirmation message (which must include information confirming service subscription, cost as well as details of how to unsubscribe from the service). As regards unsubscribing, a PRS Provider must permit an end-user to unsubscribe from a subscription service at any time without incurring further premium rate charges and must not suggest otherwise or promote or operate a subscription service with a minimum subscription period. It is beneficial to consumers to be provided with the opportunity and full and clear information (through promotions, subscription requests, regulatory reminders, etc) on how to unsubscribe from the service, otherwise there may be potential detriment, such as, incurring charges.

The issue of addictive design has particular resonance for ComReg's remit in the area of PRS as it relates to minors. The protection of minors is another key aspect of ComReg's PRS Code of Practice with specific rules around Children's Services (namely those that are either wholly or in part aimed at or would be reasonably considered as being particularly attractive to, children. For example, promotional material for Children's services must not contain anything which is likely to result in harm to children or which exploits their credulity, lack of experience or sense of loyalty; it must not promote the excessive use of PRS (for example by encouraging children to purchase other PRS or repeatedly use the same PRS); and it must not make direct appeals to children to buy or donate, unless the product or service or donation is one which they could reasonably be expected to afford for themselves. Competition services aimed at children must not offer cash prizes or feature long or complex rules. There are monetary thresholds applicable to Children's Services.
2.9 Overall, these issues raise the ongoing potential for consumer detriment and,
Page 9 of 12

possible harmful practices will continue to evolve. However, as discussed above, ComReg recognises that some practices (personalisation) may have positive effects for consumers. Similarly, the use of Artificial Intelligence ('AI') solutions across areas, such as, customer care and contact, cannot be considered problematic as such. ComReg recognises that AI is playing, and will continue to play, an increasing role in customer care and contact handling. AI capabilities assist in providing customer care services and can respond and resolve a specific range of customer issues autonomously across the customer service contact channels. However, there are different situations where customers still need to speak or chat to a person (or where oversight by a human customer service agent in relation to email or web form channels) is required.

2.10 ComReg considers that consumers including ECS and PRS consumers should be protected at a comparable level online as they are offline/in-person. ComReg supports the EC's aim of better protection of consumers who experience harmful online practices, noting that the DFFC has highlighted harms in digital consumer interactions, such as the exploitation of consumer vulnerabilities in ways not adequately covered by current laws.

2.11 However, ComReg considers that ECS users should continue to benefit from sector-specific measures and complementing general consumer protection law. ComReg utilises existing consumer protection provisions (such as, under the EECC and the Consumer Protection Act 2007) to pursue remedies for the betterment of ECS consumers.
Page 11 of 12

3.1 Regarding simplification, in the context of the DFA, ComReg notes that the EC's aim for legislative proposals is to improve consumer protection whilst minimising the regulatory burden (in terms of compliance costs). At this stage in the development of the EC's DFA initiative, ComReg is of the view that further clarification is needed as to what is meant by simplification. It would be beneficial if the discussion around simplification could move to specifics so that stakeholders could better understand both what it is, and what it is not. Until then, there is a natural limit to what can usefully be said about it, as its actual meaning remains unclear. Little objection can be taken to the concept of making things simpler, however answers to the questions 'what' and 'how' seem highly relevant to the drive for 'simplification'.

3.2 ComReg supports simplification where measures modernise and enhance consumer protection, where appropriate, and avoids a risk of duplication with existing sectoral frameworks (such as the EECC) to the extent that duplication may exist. ComReg is clear that simplification must not equate to deregulation.

3.3 ComReg agrees that any new proposals which are identified as being necessary and appropriate to address specific gaps in consumer protection should be proportionate and not impose unnecessary administrative burden on providers, including ECS providers. While ComReg broadly supports the EC's aim to enhance consumer protection where relevant, making rules clearer and more accessible, it should not compromise regulatory rigour.

3.4 Simplification should not lead to the weakening of valuable existing consumer protection provisions which aim for a high level of consumer protection in the electronic communications sector or risk interference with ComReg's ability to undertake compliance and enforcement activities in the Irish ECS market for the benefit of consumers. In ComReg's view, existing consumer protection regulation remains relevant and necessary and is effective. Any new rules that the EC may introduce should not negatively impact or impinge on that effectiveness.

3.5 As previously discussed, robust oversight is critical to address harmful evolving practices like those highlighted in the DFFC. Deregulation would risk weakening protections against exploitative tactics, which caused €7.9 billion in consumer detriment annually, per the DFFC, and eroding trust in digital markets. It is unfortunate that the meaning of 'simplification' has not yet been made clear. Real discussion on this issue will remain stifled to a significant degree unless and until this is advanced.

3.6 As the legislative initiative progresses, and to avoid the risk of unintended consequences, ComReg underscores the importance of the EC's approach to the consumer protection legislative framework geared to ensure that existing sectoral and horizontal complimentary consumer protection provisions are not undermined while consistency and coherence are achieved where appropriate.

3.7 ComReg proposes that key considerations (including, design of possible policy measures under the DFA, such as, method of cancellation, any default settings, as well as mode of information provision) are informed by a full impact assessment of any proposals.

3.8 The DFA should aim to reinforce coordination and enforcement capabilities, clearly establishing the role of competent authorities and/or national regulatory authorities (NRAs). Its focus should be to design a clear legal framework ensuring that sectoral provisions are applied consistently, while unfair practices in the broader digital ecosystem are tackled in a coordinated and coherent way across Member States.
3.9 ComReg cooperates and liaises as appropriate with other regulatory bodies in Ireland in the digital sphere and inputs to the work of BEREC.
Page 12 of 12
